receipts.quote: overlap mem copying with compute to hide latency. We present the concept in the context of GEMM (matmul), but it's applicable everywhere

node.text: ◆ signal: CUTLASS Tutorial: GEMM with Pipelining (Hieu Pham) — Software pipelining to overlap memcpy with compute is central to batched Cholesky perf, since the panel-update GEMM/SYRK inner loops dominate runtime on large n and must hide global-memory latency on A100/H100.

node.text: ◆ signal: Batched one-sided factorizations of tiny matrices using GPUs (MAGMA) — For tiny matrices (n=32-128, the high-batch entries), assign exactly one CUDA thread block per matrix, read the matrix column-by-column for coalesced access, and keep it register/shared-resident — this directly maps to the leaderboard's thousands-of-small-matrices regime.

receipts.quote: the practical vehicle for writing the fused block-Cholesky kernel

node.text: ◆ signal: cuSOLVERDx Execution Operators (Thread/Block, BatchesPerBlock) — cuSOLVERDx offers a device-side POTRF with Thread operator for MxN<=100 register-resident factorization and BatchesPerBlock to pack multiple small matrices per block — a ready-made, tunable building block for the n=32..128 batched entries without writing the kernel from scratch.

receipts.quote: the panel-update GEMM/SYRK inner loops dominate runtime on large n and must hide global-memory latency

node.text: ◆ signal: High-performance Cholesky factorization for GPU-only execution — The fork-join model — sequential memory-bound POTF2 panel factorizations interleaved with parallel Level-3 SYRK/TRSM trailing updates — means performance tracks the underlying GEMM/SYRK; for large single n (8192-32768) the panel is θ(n²) vs θ(n³) total, so optimize the trailing update and overlap TRSM on the same stream to preserve dependency.

receipts.quote: a scaffold for the shared-memory tiling and warp-level primitives needed to implement the small-batch resident Cholesky kernel

markdown:
# Idea Brief — GPU MODE — Batched Dense Cholesky Factorization (leaderboard 776)

> Generated by your counterfactual learned self: 14 of your unread saves read, 15 signals scouted outward from them. Every idea is anchored — seeds are your own saves (`papiers read <id>`), discoveries are linked.

This is the researcher's task-to-mechanism inversion: a builder who insists on understanding the mechanism (karpathy north-star), whose corpus was almost entirely LLM-agentic-loops but whose unread saves are a coherent GPU-systems curriculum — CUDA course, CUTLASS/CuTe pipelining, Ultra-Scale Playbook, GPU roofline visualization. The leaderboard is not one problem but a set of distinct shape regimes, and the corpus's own instinct — roofline/bottleneck framing before writing code (Fleetwood), pipeline schedules for peak-memory vs throughput (their PP saves) — maps almost one-to-one onto the batched-Cholesky regime split. The dominant lesson from the scouted literature (MAGMA, ICL fork-join, Dongarra batched) is: there is no single kernel; win by autotuning a crossover between a register/shared-resident one-block-per-matrix kernel (tiny-n, thousands of matrices, memory-bound) and a classical native blocked panel-factor + trailing-SYRK path (large single n, compute-bound), with a fused kernel for the medium band. Ranking below is by expected leaderboard EV given the geometric-mean scoring, which rewards not losing badly on ANY shape.

## 1. Two-kernel dispatch on a per-shape crossover, mirroring MAGMA's own dispatch logic · strong

**Approach.** Build a dispatcher that routes by (batch, n) exactly as MAGMA does: for n<=256 use a fused one-block-per-matrix resident kernel; for n in 256..2048 batched use a blocked-per-block POTRF; for n>=2048 (single/low-batch) drop the batched path entirely and run a classical native right-looking blocked Cholesky (unblocked POTF2 panel + TRSM + trailing SYRK/GEMM) with recnb fixed at 64/128. Autotune the block size nb per-n via a small offline sweep, storing a lookup table. The geometric-mean scoring punishes any single bad shape, so the dispatch table IS the product.

**Why it fits.** The researcher already thinks in terms of distinct regimes needing distinct schedules (1F1B for peak-memory vs Chimera/DualPipe for throughput) and in roofline terms (memory-bound vs compute-bound). This idea is the direct translation of that instinct: tiny matrices are memory-bound and want block-per-matrix residency; the 32768 matrix is compute-bound and wants BLAS3. Their builder ethos — ideas-shipped should equal ideas-had, ship to the leaderboard — favors a dispatch table over one clever kernel.

**Seeded by your saves:** [hl_84ff1a25c687d47909cdc3724dceefb4] ("Pipeline-parallel schedules — 1F1B for peak-memory, Chimera/BitPipe/DualPipe for bi-direct"); [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] ("Understanding GPU bottlenecks is easy with a visualisation"); [hl_cbe9c978e7088041b821296e8fac7e7d] ("ideas-had vs ideas-shipped should be 100%")
**Discovered en route:** [MAGMA spotrf_batched crossover logic — batched warns above n=2048, routes n>crossover to lg_batched](https://github.com/maxhutch/magma/blob/master/src/dpotrf_batched.cpp); [MAGMA get_nb / recnb per-n tuning parameters](https://icl.utk.edu/projectsfiles/magma/doxygen/group__magma__tuning.html); [High-performance Cholesky for GPU-only execution (fork-join model)](https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2017/icl-utk-987-2017.pdf)

**Your learning path:**
1. doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6 — Understanding GPU bottlenecks via visualization (Fleetwood) — Establishes the roofline vocabulary to classify each benchmark shape as memory- or compute-bound before choosing a kernel — the classification that drives the whole dispatch table.
2. MAGMA spotrf_batched crossover logic (github maxhutch/magma) — Shows the concrete n=2048 threshold and lg_batched routing — the empirical basis for where to put the crossover boundaries.
3. MAGMA get_nb / recnb tuning params (ICL doxygen) — Confirms per-n block-size autotuning is standard practice and gives starting nb/recnb values to seed the offline sweep.
4. ICL-UTK GPU-only Cholesky fork-join paper — Grounds the large-n branch: performance tracks the trailing SYRK/GEMM, so the dispatcher must optimize that path, not the O(n^2) panel.

## 2. cuSOLVERDx device-side POTRF as the batched building block — skip hand-writing the panel loop · strong

**Approach.** For the tiny/medium batched entries (n=32..1024), use cuSOLVERDx's device-side POTRF operators rather than writing raw CUDA. Use the Thread operator with BatchesPerBlock to pack several tiny matrices per block for n=32..64 high-batch (4096x32, 1024x64), and the blocked_potrf out-of-core left-looking path (one block per matrix: NB steps of unblocked POTRF + TRSM + cuBLASDx GEMM) for n=256..1024. Tune BatchesPerBlock and TileSize per shape. This gets a competitive baseline shipped fast, then hand-optimize only the shapes where the profile shows headroom.

**Why it fits.** The researcher's stance is execution-is-the-bottleneck and ship-first (GTFOL), and they annotate for how much scaffolding is provided beforehand — cuSOLVERDx is exactly the pre-built scaffolding that lets them cover 10+ benchmark shapes without writing a panel loop, freeing effort for the large-n kernel that actually differentiates. Karpathy-style mechanism understanding is preserved because cuSOLVERDx exposes the Thread/Block/BatchesPerBlock knobs explicitly.

**Seeded by your saves:** [hl_d29a6b5ead79fa8caf81e64ee6521d62] ("how much scaffolding is provided beforehand?"); [hl_503a1f9cf210dc18a1b27c93dccd3143] ("GTFOL / ship-to-real-users ethos"); [hl_434fe33ec2e87be47dce26e21791c157] ("spending too long chasing research milestones when he should have been talking to customer")
**Discovered en route:** [cuSOLVERDx Execution Operators (Thread/Block, BatchesPerBlock)](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/api/execution_ops.html); [cuSOLVERDx potrf_batched_thread & potrf_batched_cluster examples](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/examples/linear_solve_example.html); [cuSOLVERDx blocked_potrf.cu left-looking out-of-core POTRF](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/examples/advanced_example.html)

**Your learning path:**
1. doc_8d69f17fb60edca85a929c63d72945a7 — Elliot Arledge CUDA Course — Fills the CUDA fundamentals (thread blocks, shared memory, launch config) needed to correctly parameterize BatchesPerBlock and interpret cuSOLVERDx's execution model.
2. cuSOLVERDx Execution Operators docs — The exact API surface — Thread vs Block operator, BatchesPerBlock — that maps to the tiny-n high-batch shapes.
3. cuSOLVERDx potrf_batched_thread & _cluster examples — Copy-adaptable code for the two regime endpoints (register-resident tiny, cluster-spanning large).
4. cuSOLVERDx blocked_potrf.cu advanced example — Ready-made left-looking blocked path covering the medium n=256..1024 batched band without hand-writing the panel loop.

## 3. One-block-per-matrix register/shared-resident kernel for the tiny-n high-batch regime · strong

**Approach.** For {4096,n=32},{1024,n=64},{256,n=128}: assign exactly one CUDA thread block per matrix, read the matrix column-by-column for coalesced global loads, keep the whole triangle shared/register-resident, and do the entire unblocked Cholesky (right-looking rank-1 updates via warp-level primitives) without ever spilling to global memory. Write L back once. For n=32 pack multiple matrices per block to fill occupancy. This regime is pure memory-bandwidth bound: the win is single-load, single-store, zero kernel-launch-per-step overhead.

**Why it fits.** Directly the MAGMA batched-tiny recipe, and it matches the researcher's roofline instinct: these shapes each hold 16 MiB of input, so they're bandwidth-limited and the entire game is minimizing global traffic and launch overhead. The CUDA course and WebGPU-puzzles saves are exactly the shared-memory-tiling + warp-primitive skills this needs.

**Seeded by your saves:** [doc_814dc0be263067811fc43ddfd864c613] ("WebGPU Puzzles: practice writing GPU compute kernels"); [doc_8d69f17fb60edca85a929c63d72945a7] ("CUDA Programming Course — shared-memory tiling and warp-level primitives"); [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] ("which regime is memory-bound vs compute-bound")
**Discovered en route:** [Batched one-sided factorizations of tiny matrices using GPUs (MAGMA)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1877750317311456); [A Fast Batched Cholesky Factorization on a GPU](https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf)

**Your learning path:**
1. doc_814dc0be263067811fc43ddfd864c613 — WebGPU Puzzles — Hands-on practice writing the exact kind of small resident compute kernel this needs, at zero setup cost.
2. doc_8d69f17fb60edca85a929c63d72945a7 — Elliot Arledge CUDA Course — Shared-memory tiling, coalesced column-major loads, and warp shuffle primitives for the rank-1 update.
3. MAGMA batched tiny-matrix factorization paper — The canonical one-block-per-matrix, column-by-column coalesced read recipe this idea implements.
4. A Fast Batched Cholesky (Dongarra 2014) — Justifies fusing POTF2+TRSM into one kernel to kill launch overhead — the key move for n=128.

## 4. Software-pipelined trailing-update mainloop for the single huge matrix (n=8192..32768) · promising

**Approach.** For the one-matrix entries, the runtime is θ(n^3) in the trailing SYRK/GEMM while the panel is θ(n^2). Implement a right-looking blocked Cholesky where the panel factor (POTF2+TRSM) is small and the trailing symmetric-rank-k update dominates. Use software pipelining (CUTLASS/CuTe) to overlap the next panel's global-memory loads with the current trailing update's compute, hiding HBM latency. Critical constraint: fp32 Cholesky must use fp32 CUDA-core MMA or tf32 — the SM90 wgmma+TMA template is fp16/tensor-core, so adapt the producer-consumer pipeline structure but keep fp32 accumulation to pass the FP32 reconstruction-residual check. Keep TRSM on the same stream to preserve the dependency without stalling.

**Why it fits.** This is the single most differentiating shape on the board and squarely uses the researcher's freshest systems saves — the CUTLASS pipelining tutorial (overlap memcpy with compute to hide latency) and the CuTe terminology guide are literally the vehicle. Their pipeline-parallelism thread already primes the overlap-compute-with-communication mental model; here it's overlap-load-with-compute at the kernel level.

**Seeded by your saves:** [doc_5f11c747ee974d16234fa90c6b91cb9c] ("software pipelining: overlap mem copying with compute to hide latency... if you don't use "); [doc_44ae53055d24b1ee5ec5f7b995c7ec6e] ("CuTe layout/atom/tile abstractions — Atoms, Traits, Layouts, Collectives"); [hl_380e54be15b444cd3fe52238b00e27c9] ("pipeline schedules for bi-directional overlap")
**Discovered en route:** [CuTe SM90 wgmma+TMA pipelined mainloop (adapt structure, keep fp32)](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/blob/main/examples/cute/tutorial/hopper/wgmma_tma_sm90.cu); [High-performance Cholesky for GPU-only execution — trailing-update dominance](https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2017/icl-utk-987-2017.pdf)

**Your learning path:**
1. doc_5f11c747ee974d16234fa90c6b91cb9c — CUTLASS GEMM with Pipelining (Hieu Pham) — The core technique — overlapping global-memory copy with compute — that determines whether the n=32768 trailing update achieves peak throughput.
2. doc_44ae53055d24b1ee5ec5f7b995c7ec6e — CuTe Terminology Guide — Decodes the Atom/Layout/Tile abstractions needed to actually write the tiled trailing-SYRK kernel in CuTe.
3. CuTe SM90 wgmma+TMA mainloop example — Concrete producer-consumer PipelineState template to adapt — with the caveat to swap fp16 tensor-core for fp32/tf32 CUDA-core MMA.
4. ICL-UTK GPU-only Cholesky paper — Confirms trailing update dominates for large n, so pipelining effort is correctly targeted.

## 5. Warp Tile block-Cholesky as fast prototype, then autotune before dropping to CuTe · promising

**Approach.** Before writing raw CUTLASS, prototype the blocked panel-factor + trailing-update in NVIDIA Warp's Tile API (launch_tiled, block reading, padded_n) — a Python-accessible blocked Cholesky. Use it to validate the algorithm's numerics against the property-based checker (lower-triangular, positive diagonal, FP32 residual) and to sweep block sizes cheaply, THEN port only the hot shapes to hand-tuned CuTe. This de-risks the large-n kernel: get correctness and a block-size table in Python-time, spend CUDA-time only on the pipelined mainloop.

**Why it fits.** Matches the researcher's counterfactual-learned workflow exactly: their repo is a 'seed -> scout fan-out -> idea arsenal -> experiment race' engine, and prototyping in a fast, readable API before committing to raw kernels IS the experiment-race discipline applied to GPU code. It also honors research-as-leisure / low-friction iteration — validate cheaply, ship what wins.

## 6. Fused POTF2+TRSM single-kernel for the medium batched band (n=256..512) to kill launch overhead · promising

**Approach.** For {64,n=256},{16,n=512},{640,n=512}: the matrices are too big to be fully register-resident but small enough that per-step kernel launches (separate POTF2, TRSM, SYRK launches per block-column) dominate. Merge the panel POTF2 and TRSM into a single fused kernel per block-step so the block reads its panel once, factors and triangular-solves in shared memory, and only the trailing SYRK crosses to a second kernel. This is the regime where fusion, not raw FLOPs, wins — recursive-blocked fused kernels hit ~80% of GEMM peak per the Dongarra result.

**Why it fits.** The researcher's corpus explicitly values fusion and cache/launch-overhead minimization — they endorsed prefix-cache-friendly loops to keep repeated work cheap and annotated the offset-read trick as avoiding reading the whole thing at once. Same instinct at the kernel level: read the panel once, fuse the dependent ops, cross to global only for the trailing update. This band is the least glamorous but geometric-mean scoring makes it as valuable as any other entry.

**Discovered en route:** [A Fast Batched Cholesky — merging panel POTF2 and TRSM into a single fused kernel](https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf); [cuSOLVERDx blocked_potrf.cu (reference for the fused per-block structure)](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/examples/advanced_example.html)

**Your learning path:**

2. doc_5f11c747ee974d16234fa90c6b91cb9c — CUTLASS GEMM with Pipelining — Even the fused medium kernel's trailing SYRK benefits from load/compute overlap; teaches how to keep the panel resident while streaming the update.
3. cuSOLVERDx blocked_potrf.cu — Reference implementation of the per-block panel loop to fuse and benchmark against.

## 7. Exploit the special input structure (diagonal / tridiagonal / low-rank) with a fast-path branch · speculative

**Approach.** The inputs include diagonal matrices, tridiagonal SPD, and damped low-rank matrices. A diagonal matrix's Cholesky is just elementwise sqrt of the diagonal; tridiagonal SPD admits an O(n) scalar recurrence rather than O(n^3) blocked factorization. Add a cheap structure-detection prefilter (check off-diagonal sparsity / bandwidth in one pass) and branch to a specialized O(n) or O(nk) kernel when structure is detected, falling back to the general blocked path otherwise. Since ranking is geometric-mean runtime, collapsing even a few shapes to near-free multiplies the overall score.

**Why it fits.** This is the researcher's mechanism-first, exploit-the-structure instinct applied literally — and it rewards their taste for cheap knobs that swing results (they endorsed reasoning-effort tuning as a cheap knob to sweep). The risk is the checker uses cond as a dynamic-range control and structure may not be reliably detectable cheaply; hence speculative. But if even the diagonal-matrix shapes are common in the grid, an elementwise-sqrt fast path is nearly free and pure upside.
